source.title: Liquid Nitrogen Fatal Release at Foundation Food Group

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Liquid Nitrogen Release
event.incident_type: Cryogenic liquid release resulting in asphyxiation fatalities
actions_taken: Emergency response by local fire department
Investigation by U.S. Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board (CSB)
event.summary: On January 28, 2021, liquid nitrogen overflowed from an immersion freezer at Foundation Food Group's Plant 4 in Gainesville, Georgia. The overflow occurred while maintenance workers were troubleshooting operational issues with the freezer, which was designed and owned by Messer LLC and rented to FFG. Once released, the liquid nitrogen vaporized, expanded, and rapidly accumulated within a partially enclosed cold room lacking mechanical ventilation. Two maintenance workers died from asphyxiation due to vaporized nitrogen. The uncontrolled nitrogen leak continued undetected for 30-60 minutes. When discovered, at least 14 FFG employees responded by entering the cold room or attempting rescue, resulting in four additional fatalities and three employees plus one firefighter suffering severe asphyxiation symptoms. The incident was caused by failure of the liquid level control system due to deformation of the bubble-type level gauge component, combined with inadequate process safety management, emergency preparedness, atmospheric monitoring, and product stewardship practices.
recommendations: Gold Creek Foods (current facility owner) should proactively engage local emergency responders with information about hazardous substances, critical equipment locations, and emergency systems
Messer LLC should update product stewardship policy to include participation in customer PHAs, verify proper signage for oxygen-deficient atmospheres, and review emergency shutdown switch locations
Messer should create informational materials for customers on safety issues and effective safety management systems for inert gas asphyxiation hazards
OSHA should update Regional Emphasis Programs for poultry processing facilities (Regions 4 and 6) and food manufacturing (Region 5) to explicitly cover liquid nitrogen freezing processes

OSHA should promulgate a specific standard for cryogenic asphyxiant agents addressing equipment design, atmospheric monitoring, emergency shutdown systems, employee training, emergency action plans, and process safety management elements
OSHA should develop guidance document for process safety management practices applicable to compressed gases and cryogenic asphyxiant agents
Compressed Gas Association (CGA) should develop comprehensive standard for safe storage, handling, and use of liquid nitrogen in fixed facilities

CGA should update P-76 standard on oxygen-deficient atmospheres to require atmospheric monitoring systems, distinct alarm indications, remote-operated emergency isolation valves (ROEIV), and guidance on emergency stop device locations

National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) should update NFPA 55 to require atmospheric monitoring with cryogenic asphyxiant agents and include guidance on safe location of manual isolation valves and emergency activation devices

International Code Council (ICC) should update International Fire Code to require atmospheric monitoring with cryogenic asphyxiant agents and include guidance on safe location of isolation valves and emergency devices

threats.name: Bubble-type level gauge deformation during maintenance
Loss of liquid level measurement and control
Uncontrolled liquid nitrogen overflow from immersion freezer
Rapid vaporization and expansion of nitrogen gas in enclosed space
Accumulation of nitrogen gas in partially enclosed cold room
Displacement of oxygen creating oxygen-deficient atmosphere
Undetected oxygen-deficient atmosphere due to lack of monitoring
Inadequate emergency preparedness and response procedures

consequences.name: Worker asphyxiation and loss of consciousness
Multiple rescue attempts in hazardous atmosphere

controls.name: Bubble-type level gauge design and redundancy
controls.barrier_role: Primary liquid level measurement and control device

supporting_text: The bubble-type level gauge probably bent during maintenance activity, preventing it from measuring and controlling the liquid nitrogen level in the freezer
The design was vulnerable to a single point of failure. Once the gauge bent, there was nothing else to prevent the liquid nitrogen from leaking

During manufacturing of the immersion freezer, Messer failed to detect a manufacturing defect that exacerbated the likelihood of the bubble-type level gauge bending: a clamp intended to secure the bubble-type level gauge to the freezer was missing

controls.name: Immersion freezer design with redundant level control safeguards
controls.barrier_role: Prevent overflow through multiple protective layers
controls.lod_basis: Design should prevent single component failure from causing loss of containment

supporting_text: The design team did not adequately consider the consequences of the bubble-type level gauge failure (a critical safety component) and did not identify appropriate safeguards to mitigate its possible failure
After the incident, Messer revised the design of its freezer to include multiple layers of protection against liquid nitrogen overflow

controls.name: Manufacturing quality control and inspection procedures
controls.barrier_role: Detect manufacturing defects before equipment operation
controls.lod_basis: Prevent defective equipment from being deployed

supporting_text: During manufacturing of the immersion freezer, Messer failed to detect a manufacturing defect: a clamp intended to secure the bubble-type level gauge to the freezer was missing

After the incident, Messer reviewed its quality control process and procedures to require verification of the presence of necessary bubble-type level gauge clamps and reordered inspection steps to facilitate this verification

controls.name: Atmospheric monitoring system in cold room
controls.barrier_role: Detect oxygen-deficient atmosphere and alert personnel
controls.lod_basis: Provide early warning of hazardous atmosphere to prevent exposure

supporting_text: FFG did not install atmospheric monitoring equipment in the cold room
No equipment was installed to detect the oxygen-deficient atmosphere in the cold room, automatically shut off the liquid nitrogen supply, and notify personnel to evacuate the area
There is abundant industry guidance on the importance of atmospheric monitoring when there is a possibility of hazardous atmospheres occurring

controls.name: Mechanical ventilation system in cold room
controls.barrier_role: Dilute and remove nitrogen gas accumulation

supporting_text: The cold room lacked mechanical ventilation
The cold room had no HVAC, although the immersion freezers contained exhaust systems designed to remove vaporized nitrogen from normal freezer operation

controls.name: Emergency action plan and employee training
controls.barrier_role: Prepare workforce to recognize hazards and respond safely
controls.lod_basis: Prevent unsafe rescue attempts and exposure

supporting_text: FFG did not inform, train, equip, drill, or otherwise prepare its workforce for a liquid nitrogen leak

FFG's workforce lacked knowledge about the hazards of nitrogen and its ability to create an oxygen-deficient atmosphere, could not recognize an oxygen-deficient atmosphere, and lacked any apparatus or PPE that would have permitted safe entry into an oxygen-deficient atmosphere
After discovery of the first two deceased workers, four additional employees who entered the cold room also died from asphyxiation
At least 14 FFG employees responded to the incident by entering the cold room or attempting rescue

controls.name: Process Safety Management system
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and implement appropriate controls
controls.lod_basis: Systematic approach to hazard identification and risk reduction

supporting_text: FFG did not have a documented process safety management policy
FFG allowed the position responsible for safety management to remain vacant for more than one year before the incident
FFG did not evaluate the process risks associated with the freezer
FFG lacked written procedures and a management of change process
FFG did not train its workers about the asphyxiation hazards of liquid nitrogen
Despite strong industry guidance on process safety management systems, the law did not require FFG to implement them

controls.name: Product stewardship and supplier oversight
controls.barrier_role: Ensure customer implements adequate safety practices
controls.lod_basis: Supplier responsibility to prevent unsafe operations

supporting_text: Messer was the owner of the bulk liquid nitrogen storage tanks and the immersion spiral freezer and rented the equipment to FFG

Messer had knowledge, experience, policies, and institutional practices for effective product stewardship, but applied those practices only to the bulk storage tanks and not to the Line 4 freezing process
Throughout its relationship with FFG, Messer had identified problems with FFG's safety practices and non-compliance with industry guidance
Despite FFG's unsafe practices, Messer continued to supply liquid nitrogen to FFG
If Messer had suspended service until FFG corrected known safety deficiencies, this incident might have been prevented

controls.name: Emergency shutdown systems and isolation valves

supporting_text: The system was designed with safety interlocks that could automatically prevent involuntary and dangerous nitrogen liquid leaks by activating through the control system measuring a high liquid level or by pressing emergency stop buttons located outside the freezer
The uncontrolled nitrogen leak continued for 30 to 60 minutes undetected
FFG did not proactively interact with local first responders before the incident, despite depending on them to respond to emergencies at its facility

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields are evidence-based. null used for unknown values.
